coredata.dc:title: Antibacterial fluorescent nano-sized lanthanum-doped carbon quantum dot embedded polyvinyl alcohol for accelerated wound healing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Mingqian
Su, Yutian
Liu, Yihan
Liang, Ying
Wu, Shishan
Zhou, Ninglin
Shen, Jian
coredata.dc:description: Bacteria is one of the main culprits that cause human diseases and pose long-term challenges to people’s health. Rare earth elements have unique antibacterial advantages, but little research is available. In this paper, we reported an antibacterial composite film based on lanthanum-doped carbon quantum dot nanoparticles (La@N-P-CQDs) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film for fluorescence of antibiotics and accelerating wound healing. PVA/La@N-P-CQDs composite film presented excellent hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, fluorescence intensity, and antibacterial effects. The antibacterial activity of La@N-P-CQDs was evaluated by employing antibacterial assay using Escherichia coli (E.coli)and Staphylococcus aureus (S.aureus) in vitro. La@N-P-CQDs showed enhanced antibacterial activity compared with N-P-CQDs. Moreover, the PVA/La@N-P-CQDs composite film with 0.5 mg/mL La@N-P-CQDs showed better antibacterial capability and wound healing performance than PVA and PVA/N-P-CQDs films in bacterial adhesion experiment. PVA/La@N-P-CQDs composite film could be used for wound dressing in vivo experiment and had no side effects on major organs in mice. The antibacterial composite film significantly promoted in vivo wound healing process because of its multifunctional properties. Therefore, it was an excellent candidate for wound dressing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite film
Wound dressing
Biocompatibility
Antibacterial effect
Wound healing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cytotoxicity, hemolysis experiment, and cell fluorescence imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Foundation of Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Foundation of Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Characterization of La@N-P-CQDs: (a) TEM (Insert is high-resolution TEM) and (b) Size of La@N-P-CQDs. (B) EDS spectrum of La@N-P-CQDs. (C) FTIR spectra of AMP and La@N-P-CQDs. (D) XRD of N-P-CQDs and La@N-P-CQDs. XPS of (E) La@N-P-CQDs, (F) C 1 s, and (G) O 1 s. (H) UV–vis absorbance spectra, max excitation, and max emission wavelength of La@N-P-CQDs (inset is the picture of La@N-P-CQDs aqueous solution under 365 nm UV light).

(A) Proliferation rate of NIH3T3 cells in the AMP and La@N-P-CQDs over 24 h. (Experiment was conducted three times). (B) Hemolysis rate of the AMP and La@N-P-CQDs with different concentration solutions (a), the optical images of RBCs from negative control (b), 100 μg/mL of the AMP (c), and 100 μg/mL of the La@N-P-CQDs (d) (scanning baseline: 30 μm). (Experiment was conducted three times). (C) Fluorescent images of A549 cells in incubation with the La@N-P-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths: 405 nm (a), 488 nm (b), and 543 nm (c) (scanning baseline: 20 μm).

(A) Minimum inhibitory concentration curve of N-P-CQDs and La@N-P-CQDs to E. coli and S. aureus (Experiment was conducted three times). Inhibitory kinetic curves of La@N-P-CQDs with the same amount of (B) E. coli and (C) S. aureus. (D) Colony status images of different concentrations of La@N-P-CQDs with liquid medium containing the same amount of E. coli and S. aureus. (E) Histograms of the antibacterial ability of La@N-P-CQDs (The experiment was repeated three times).

(A) Weight loss curves of PVA, PVA/N-P-CQDs, and PVA/La@N-P-CQDs. (B) WCA photographs of PVA, PVA/N-P-CQDs, and PVA/La@N-P-CQDs. (C) Slow-release curve of PVA/N-P-CQDs and PVA/La@N-P-CQDs. (D) Proliferation rate of NIH3T3 cells in the PVA, PVA/N-P-CQDs, and PVA/La@N-P-CQDs over 24 h. (E,F) SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus on PVA, PVA/ N-P-CQDs, and PVA/ La@N-P-CQD composite films. (The experiment was repeated three times).

(A) Photographs of wounds at 0 D, 7 D, and 14 D. (B,C) Wound area for control, PVA, PVA/N-P-CQDs, and PVA/La@N-P-CQDs (The experiment was repeated three times). (D) H&E and Masson-stained images at 14 D for control, PVA, PVA/N-P-CQDs, and PVA/La@N-P-CQDs.
Schematic illustration of the formation of (A) PVA/La@N-P-CQDs and (B) bacteria-killing processes.

head.ce:title: Antibacterial fluorescent nano-sized lanthanum-doped carbon quantum dot embedded polyvinyl alcohol for accelerated wound healing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Biomedical Functional Materials
School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we employ 5′-adenosine disodium and Lanthanum chloride to prepare lanthanum-doped carbon quantum dot by one-step hydrothermal method. Then, the La@N-P-CQD NPs was embedded in the PVA to manufacture La@N-P-CQDs/PVA film for fluorescence monitoring of antibiotics. The composite film showed better antibacterial capability and wound healing performance and had no side effects on major organs in mice.

Bacteria is one of the main culprits that cause human diseases and pose long-term challenges to people’s health. Rare earth elements have unique antibacterial advantages, but little research is available. In this paper, we reported an antibacterial composite film based on lanthanum-doped carbon quantum dot nanoparticles (La@N-P-CQDs) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film for fluorescence of antibiotics and accelerating wound healing. PVA/La@N-P-CQDs composite film presented excellent hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, fluorescence intensity, and antibacterial effects. The antibacterial activity of La@N-P-CQDs was evaluated by employing antibacterial assay using Escherichia coli (E.coli)and Staphylococcus aureus (S.aureus) in vitro. La@N-P-CQDs showed enhanced antibacterial activity compared with N-P-CQDs. Moreover, the PVA/La@N-P-CQDs composite film with 0.5 mg/mL La@N-P-CQDs showed better antibacterial capability and wound healing performance than PVA and PVA/N-P-CQDs films in bacterial adhesion experiment. PVA/La@N-P-CQDs composite film could be used for wound dressing in vivo experiment and had no side effects on major organs in mice. The antibacterial composite film significantly promoted in vivo wound healing process because of its multifunctional properties. Therefore, it was an excellent candidate for wound dressing.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections pose an increasing threat to our health. In general, bacteria attach to the surface of human skin mucous membranes with the help of some special components and structures on the surface and then cause diseases in different ways. [1–3] At present, antibiotics are mainly used to treat bacteria, leading to the emergence of many drug-resistant bacteria. This scenario brings new difficulties for bacterial infection treatment[4,5]. Moreover, accurate representation of the extent and size of wounds becomes increasingly important when the areas and tissues infected by bacteria require further monitoring. Therefore, an intelligent platform that integrates fluorescence detection and antimicrobial therapy should be developed.

Rare earth elements, which have always been known as “industrial vitamins,” are a vital resource. They have been widely used in industry, agriculture, and medical materials. Lanthanum (La) is one of the most widely studied rare earth elements because it not only is a trace element in human bones but also can inhibit cancer cell reproduction [6–8]. Moreover, it has a good antibacterial effect. La and its compounds exhibit the characteristics of a broad-spectrum, low-toxicity, and small dosage antibiotic [9,10]. La3+ interacts with the main components of peptidoglycan, phospholipid, and protein on the cell membrane surface to form a stable complex, thereby changing cell membrane permeability [11]. Thus, the accumulation of internal formation allows La3+ to bind to DNA and protein when La3+ enters the bacteria, thereby affecting the metabolism and inhibiting bacteria growth. [12,13]. Metal resistance associated with plasmids is quite common in pathogenic bacteria. Some metals can effectively produce resistance to prokaryotic plasmids (e.g., resistance to silver) and constrain bacterial growth. Therefore, La and its compounds can be added to a carrier to prepare new antibacterial materials [14–16]. In addition to good antibacterial functions, rare earth elements have excellent autofluorescence properties [17,18], provided by their f-f electronic transition. However, certain shortcomings, such as poor mechanical properties, low light stability, and thermal stability, have affected their wide application. Therefore, doping rare earth elements into nanomaterials to form multifunctional nanomaterials has attracted much attention of researchers [19–22].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), nanomaterials with a size generally below 10 nm, possess good fluorescence properties. Meanwhile, CQDs have good biocompatibility, excellent water solubility, fluorescence stability, and high fluorescence intensity; in particular CQD surface contains a large number of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy functional groups, which can be modified to obtain multifunctional nanomaterials [23–27]. However, the application of single CQDs has certain limitations and does not have an antibacterial function. Therefore, combining CQDs with other nanomaterials to form nanocomposites has become a popular research topic. He et al. compounded the rare earth element Eu3+ with CQDs and then doped them into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film to prepare light-converting nanocomposites [28]. Sun et al. used liquid phase photolysis method to wrap gold or platinum particles on the surface of PEG1500N-functionalized CQDs to form CQDs-based nanocomposites, which can achieve photoreduction of CO2 conversion [29].

As a water-soluble polymer with good biocompatibility, PVA relies on the advantages of perfect synthesis technology and easy processing and molding, which are used in biomedical dressings [30–33], light conversion materials [34], and shape memory materials [35]. However, the film made by pure PVA has a single performance, and it is difficult to be widely used in biomedical materials. Therefore, we can prepare smart composite dressings, which are prepared by the single function of PVA with nanomaterials, natural substances, and synthetic drugs for further antibacterial purposes.

Hence, in this work, a one-step hydrothermal method was used to prepare lanthanum-doped carbon quantum dot nanoparticles (La@N-P-CQDs) to prepare N-P-CQDs with antibacterial properties further. Then, the introduction of La@N-P-CQDs into a PVA film was designed and prepared to protect the wound, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . La3+ penetrated the cell membrane and entered the bacteria when the bacteria were in contact with La@N-P-CQDs. The accumulation of internal formation allowed La3+ to bind to DNA and protein, thereby affecting the metabolism and inhibiting bacteria growth by utilizing the moist condition of PVA film and the advantages of La@N-P-CQDs and PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite film, which both had antibacterial activity and wound healing capability.

Lanthanum chloride (LaCl3) was purchased from Diyang Chemical Reagent Company, Shanghai, China. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), PVA (MW 1750), and glutaraldehyde were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company, Shanghai, China. Moreover, 5′-adenosine disodium (AMP) was purchased from Ruigu Biotechnology Company, Shanghai, China. DMEM low-sugar liquid medium, PBS buffer (pH = 7.4), trypsin (0.25%), fetal bovine serum (FBS), and penicillin mixture (100X) were purchased from Hyclone Company, America. 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Company, Shanghai, China. The deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ) used for all experiments was obtained from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA). All drugs used above are analytically pure.

Transmission electron microscopy was tested on an H7650 transmission electron microscope (TEM; Japan). Dynamic light scattering was performed on Malvern Zetasizer-ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, UK). Scanning electron microscopy was performed using Ultra 55 scanning electron microscope (SEM). Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted on a Nicolet Nexus 870 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was conducted on D8 Bruker. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on PHI 5000 Versa Probe. Photoluminescence spectra were recorded using a molecular fluorescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a UV absorption spectrophotometer (Cary-50, Varian, USA). The fluorescence lifetime was measured using a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FM-4P-TCSPC, Horiba Jobin Yvon, USA). TGA was conducted on a thermal analyzer (Pyris 1, Perkin-Elmer, USA). Water contact angle (WCA) was determined from a contact angle instrument (SL200D, Kino, USA). Fluorescence imaging was conducted on a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM; SP8, Leica, Germany).

The preparation of N-P-CQDs was as follows: AMP was dissolved in 30 mL of purified water. The AMP was dissolved using a magnetic stirrer for 15 min and then added into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After being cooled at room temperature, the upper yellow solution was filtered using a Millipore filter (0.22 µm) to remove the precipitation. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min and finally freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain N-P-CQD powder.

La@N-P-CQDs were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal method. AMP (0.1 M) and LaCl3 (0.1 M) were dissolved in 40 mL of purified water. The mixture was dissolved using a magnetic stirrer for 15 min and then added into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After being cooled at room temperature, the upper yellow solution was filtered using a Millipore filter (0.22 µm) to remove the precipitation. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min and finally freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain La@N-P-CQDs powder.

The synthesized N-P-CQDs and La@N-P-CQDs were separately dissolved in purified water. PVA was dissolved in purified water and placed in a water bath at 96 °C to obtain a clear and transparent solution. N-P-CQD and La@N-P-CQDs solutions were mixed with the PVA aqueous solution. Then, the solutions were poured into a Teflon watch glass and dried at a room temperature environment to form a film.

La@N-P-CQDs cytotoxicity was determined via the MTT assay using NIH-3 T3 cells. The NIH-3 T3 cells were seeded onto different concentrations of La@N-P-CQDs solutions (0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, and 1 mg/mL) and tissue culture plate in 96-well plates with 100 μL of medium. The cells were cultured for 1, 3, and 7 days followed by incubation under a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37 °C. After days 1, 3, and 7, the culture medium was removed from each plate, and 100 μL of the MTT solution (5 mg/mL in PBS) was added into each plate with continuous culture at 37 °C for 4 h. Afterward, the MTT solution was replaced with 100 μL of DMSO and then shaken at a shaking table for 15 min to dissolve the formazan completely. Then, the optical density (OD) was measured at 570 nm. The cell viability was calculated as follows in Eq. (1):
Cell
viability ( % ) = O D
in
experimental
group / O D
in
control
group × 100 %

La@N-P-CQDs were prepared into 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 µg/mL solutions and kept at 37 °C for 30 min. The fresh rabbit blood was centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was carefully removed, and the lower red blood cells (RBCs) were washed with PBS. Then, the washed RBCs were diluted with physiological saline. An amount of 10 mL of La@N-P-CQDs solution of different concentrations was added to a 15 mL centrifuge tube. Then, 200 µL of diluted RBCs were added, shaken well, and placed in a 37 °C constant temperature incubator for 3 h. Centrifugation at 1500 rpm for 10 min followed. The supernatant was taken out, and its absorbance was tested at a wavelength of 545 nm. For the negative control group, 200 μL of diluted RBCs were added to 10 mL of physiological saline. The hemolysis rate was calculated as follows in Eq. (2):
Hemolysis
rate ( % ) = ( ODt - ODnc ) / ( ODpc - ODnc ) × 100 %

Fresh rabbit blood was centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was carefully removed. The RBCs were washed with PBS, incubated for 10 min at room temperature with physiological saline, 100 μg/mL AMP, and 100 μg/mL La@N-P-CQDs solution, and then dissolved in PBS. Afterward, the RBCs were fixed for 2 h with 4% formaldehyde and subjected to a dehydration gradient with ethanol solutions (74, 84, 94, and 100%, v/v).

The well-growing human lung cancer cell A549 cells were taken out. The culture flask under the alcohol lamp was opened. The original culture medium was discarded, and 2 mL PBS buffer was added to wash the surface of the cells twice. Then, the PBS was discarded. After 1 mL of trypsin digestion solution was added for full digestion, 10% FBS DMEM culture solution was drawn to terminate the digestion. The cells were gently blown off the bottle wall repeatedly to form a uniform single-cell suspension. Then, 1 mL of the cell suspension was aspirated in a Petri dish and placed in a 37 °C CO2 incubator for 12 h. The original culture solution was discarded, and 0.5 mg/mL La@N-P-CQDs prepared with DMEM culture solution were added. After standing for 6 h in a CO2 incubator, the original La@N-P-CQDs solution was discarded and washed three times with PBS buffer. An appropriate amount of 5% paraformaldehyde solution was added and placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C overnight. The CLSM was used to observe the cell fluorescence state under bright field, 405, 488, and 543 nm excitation.

First, we prepared 4 mL of La@N-P-CQDs solutions of different concentrations (2.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.25, and 0.125 mg/mL). Then, 40 μL of activated E. coli and S. aureus was drawn, and 4 mL of sample solutions of different concentrations was added. The culture was shaken for 24 h in a constant temperature incubator at 37 °C. Then, we drew 200 μL of the sample solution containing bacteria, added them to 96-well plates, and tested its OD value at 570 nm wavelength.

First, we prepared 4 mL of La@N-P-CQDs solutions of different concentrations (2.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.25, and 0.125 mg/mL). We drew 40 μL of activated E. coli and S. aureus and added 4 mL of sample solutions of different concentrations. Then, we drew 200 μL of sample solutions containing bacteria and added them to 96-well plates. We used a microplate reader to measure the OD value of the sample every 10 min at a wavelength of 570 nm. Continuous measurement for 24 h was performed, and the kinetic curve of bacterial growth was drawn.

Two bacteria, E. coli (Gram-negative) and S. aureus (Gram-positive), were used to evaluate the antibacterial activity of N-P-CQDs and La@N-P-CQDs via spread plate method. The samples of different concentrations (2.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.25, and 0.125 mg/mL) were cocultivated with E. coli and S. aureus for 24 h and then diluted 105 times with sterilized DI water. We drew 100 μL of the diluted liquid and spread it evenly on the surface of the solid medium containing agar. The samples were shaken for 24 h in a constant temperature incubator at 37 °C. Then, we observed the number of colonies that formed on the surface of the medium and took pictures. The antibacterial ratio was calculated as follows in Eq (3):
Antibacterial
Ratio ( % ) = ( cell
count
of
control
group - survivor
count
on
experimental
group ) / cell
count
of
control
group × 100 %

N-P-CQDs/PVA and PVA/La@N-P-CQDs films were cut into 1 × 3 cm diaphragms, immersed in a PBS solution (pH = 7.4), and placed in a 37 °C shaking constant temperature incubator. At the set time point, we drew 3 mL of the extract and simultaneously added 3 mL of fresh PBS buffer. The absorbance of the collected extract was measured at 250 nm, and a slow-release curve was drawn.

The PVA, PVA/N-P-CQDs, and PVA/La@N-P-CQDs film were cut into a 10 mm diameter film and placed in a clean UV lamp for 1 h and then in a 24-well plate. Afterward, 2 mL of liquid medium was added, and 20 μL of activated E. coli and S. aureus bacterial solution was cultured in a 37 °C shaking incubator for 24 h. The three kinds of film were washed three times with PBS. Then, the bacteria on the film were fixed with 0.5 mL of 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution (v/v, diluted with PBS). Finally, 50%, 60%, 70%, 80%, 90%, and 100% gradient ethanol solutions were dehydrated for 30 min. The filmswere placed on a clean bench and air dried. The gold was sprayed, and the bacteria that adhered to the film surface were observed using SEM.

The healthy male Wistar mice were purchased from Nanjing Sikerui Biological Technology Co Ltd. All animal operations were conducted according to the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee guidelines. The mice were individually raised in cages at a standard temperature for 3 days and randomly divided into three groups with six mice in each group: PVA group, PVA/N-P-CQDs composite film group, and PVA/La@N-P-CQDs composite film group. The dorsal area of the rat was totally depilated after the anesthesia with 10% chloral hydrate (30 mg kg−1) was administered, and one full-thickness square wound (approximately 6 mm in side length) was created on the upper back of each rat. Afterward, the prepared wounds were treated with 10 μL of S. aureus (107 CFU mL−1). After 12 h, the infection was formed and covered with samples. The wounds of all mice were tightly wrapped with nonopaque sterile medical tapes, and the mice were individually raised in cages at a standard temperature. Each dressing was changed every 2 days, and the process continued for 14 days. After 7 and 14 days, the wounds were examined and photographed. After 3, 7, and 14 days, the wound site with the surrounding skin was harvested and fixed with 10% formalin. The central wound sections were fixed on glass slides and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). Finally, the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were harvested and stained with H&E after 14 days of treatment. H&E staining was used to evaluate the amount of adherent bacteria around the wounds and wound-healing progress.
Wound
area
ratio ( % ) =
S
7 ,
14
in
experimental
group / S 0
in
control
group × 100 %

All of the data were expressed as mean ± standard deviations. Each test was repeated three or more times. Analysis of variance statistics was performed, and p values less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

La@N-P-CQDs were prepared according to Scheme 1. The prepared La@N-P-CQDs had good dispersion, relatively uniform size, and approximately spherical shape (Fig. 1A). The inset in Fig. 1A, a, was the particle size distribution of La@N-P-CQDs. The particle size distribution was 1.25–4.25 nm, and the average particle size was 2.55 nm.

Fig. 1 B and S1 show that both AMP and La@N-P-CQDs contained C, N, O, and P elements, and the carbon content of La@N-P-CQDs was significantly higher than that of AMP. The La content of La@N-P-CQDs was approximately 2.62%. Compared with the EDS images of AMP, the EDS images of La@N-P-CQDs showed the characteristic absorption peaks of La at 4.64, 5.03, and 5.36 keV, which were the characteristic absorption peaks of La4d, La3d5/2, and La3d3/2, respectively. Therefore, La had successfully combined with N-P-CQDs to produce La@N-P-CQDs.

The FTIR spectrum of AMP and La@N-P-CQDs (Fig. 1C) showed that the peak shape changed to a certain extent when LaCl3 reacted with AMP to generate La@N-P-CQDs. The absorption peak of AMP at 3350 cm−1 had a slight splitting, and two sharp absorption peaks of La@N-P-CQDs appeared at 3363 and 3143 cm−1; these findings were attributed to the stretching vibration absorption peak of OH and-NH2, respectively. The characteristic absorption peak at 1670 cm−1 was attributed to CO stretching vibration. The characteristic absorption peak at 1600 cm−1 was attributed to NH bending vibration. The absorption peak at 1400 cm−1 was attributed to the CH deformation vibration. Additionally, the absorption peaks at 1300 and 1103 cm−1 were attributed to the PO stretching vibration and POC deformation vibration, respectively. Moreover, some absorption peaks disappeared and weakened after AMP reacted with LaCl3. The reason is that La3+ coordinated with the oxygen atom on the AMP to generate La@N-P-CQDs after a high-temperature hydrothermal reaction, thereby changing the infrared absorption. Fig. 1D shows that compared with the diffraction peaks of N-P-CQDs, the interlayer spacing of La@N-P-CQDs had slightly increased, and the intensity of the diffraction peaks had been significantly reduced. The reason is that the coordination of AMP and La3+ changed the internal structure of N-P-CQDs under high-temperature carbonization and cracking, resulting in the change in diffraction peaks.

The surface composition and element analysis of La@N-P-CQDs were analyzed using X-ray electron spectroscopy. The characteristic peaks at 284.2, 400.1, 531.3, and 133.6 eV were attributed to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and P 2p, respectively (Fig. 1E). The characteristic peak at 105.2 eV was attributed to La4d, whereas the characteristic peaks at 837.3 and 855.2 eV were attributed to La3d. Moreover, the content of La was 2.16%; thus, La had been compounded with N-P-CQDs to form La@N-P-CQDs. In Fig. 1F, the characteristic peaks at 284.2, 285.1, and 288.4 eV were attributed to CC/CC (sp2), CN/CO (sp3), and CO/CN, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 397.9, 398.6, 399.4, and 400.0 eV were attributed to HN (sp3), CNC/CN, and C3-N, respectively (Figure S2). In Fig. 1G, the characteristic peaks at 531.8 and 530.9 eV were attributed to COC/COH and O2−, respectively. These results indicated that La@N-P-CQDs contained a large amount of COO−, OH, and NH2 −, which was consistent with the FTIR data. La@N-P-CQDs were successfully generated by one-step hydrothermal method.

The UV–vis absorbance spectra of La@N-P-CQDs had a strong absorption peak at 250 nm, which was mainly caused by the π-π* electronic transition (Fig. 1H). Therefore, the structure of La@N-P-CQDs contained CC conjugate. However, compared with that of N-P-CQDs (Figure S3), the absorption peak of La@N-P-CQDs at 340 nm disappeared, indicating that the surface structure of La@N-P-CQDs had changed significantly. Moreover, La@N-P-CQDs presented maximum excitation and emission peaks at 360 and 440 nm, respectively, in an aqueous solution. The fluorescence spectra of La@N-P-CQD NP aqueous solution under short- and long-wavelength excitations were tested to explore further the photoluminescence performance of La@N-P-CQDs. The excitation wavelength dependence of La@N-P-CQDs was more obvious than that of N-P-CQDs, and the emission wavelength and redshift distance of the former were increased compared with those of the latter (Figures S4 and S5). Moreover, the upconversion fluorescence of La@N-P-CQDs was more obvious, and its fluorescence performance was better than those of N-P-CQDs (Figure S6). These results were observed mainly because the doping of La changed the surface traps of N-P-CQDs and increased the electron generation. Moreover, La@N-P-CQDs had an f orbital transition, which enhanced photon absorption and energy conversion efficiency. The fluorescence intensity of La@N-P-CQDs reached the maximum when the concentration of La@N-P-CQD NP aqueous solution was 1 mg/mL (Figure S7A). Figure S7B shows that the average fluorescence lifetime of La@N-P-CQDs at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm was 4.18 ns. Therefore, La@N-P-CQDs would not be forbidden during the transition, and the luminescence performance was good, which was beneficial to the maintenance of fluorescence during the fluorescent labeling of cells. Figure S8 showed that the emission wavelength position of La@N-P-CQDs had not changed and could be used as potential materials in pH 6–8. Figure S9A showed that the fluorescence intensity of La@N-P-CQDs solutions with different concentrations of NaCl did not change significantly. This finding indicated that La@N-P-CQDs were stable, thereby laying the foundation for subsequent experiments. Moreover, La@N-P-CQDs had good photobleaching resistance and could withstand long-term UV irradiation (Figure S9B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity, hemolysis experiment, and cell fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibility of La@N-P-CQDs is vital for antibacterial applications. The cytotoxicity data statistics of AMP and La@N-P-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 A and S10. The comparison of the relative cell proliferation rates of 24 and 48 h indicated that AMP and La@N-P-CQDs were both above 95%. Moreover, the cell proliferation rate of La@N-P-CQDs was relatively high at low concentrations; this finding was mainly related to the Hormesis effect of rare earth elements [7]. Therefore, AMP and La@N-P-CQDs had no obvious cytotoxicity and could be used in biomaterial fields, such as cell fluorescence or cell detection.

Furthermore, the hemolysis rate of AMP and La@N-P-CQDs at different concentrations was also analyzed, as shown in Fig. 2B. The hemolysis rate of AMP and La@N-P-CQDs was lower than 5%, as shown in Fig. 2B, indicating that AMP and La@N-P-CQDs had a small destructive effect on RBCs. This finding met the standards of biomedical materials. According to the RBC morphology, the RBCs were evenly distributed, and the morphology was maintained well. The addition of AMP and La@N-P-CQDs did not cause RBC rupture, further indicating that La@N-P-CQDs have good biocompatibility.

Fig. 2C shows that the density of A549 cells was uniform, and the cell membrane was intact under a CLSM. La@N-P-CQDs-labeled A549 cells emitted different colors of light under the excitation of different excitation wavelengths. La@N-P-CQDs showed multicolor fluorescence when labeling cells, mainly resulting in their excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence characteristics. In addition to showing different fluorescence colors in the cytoplasm, La@N-P-CQDs showed different fluorescence colors in the nucleus, indicating that La@N-P-CQDs had entered the nucleus; this finding had great significance in the biological field [36,37].

The increase in La@N-P-CQDs concentration significantly decreased the absorbance of the figures, indicating that the concentrations of E. coli and S. aureus were decreasing. Thus, La@N-P-CQDs had obvious bactericidal effect on E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. 3 A). When the concentration of La@N-P-CQDs reached 0.5 mg/mL, the concentration of E. coli and S. aureus reached the low level, significantly inhibiting proliferation. Therefore, the minimum inhibitory concentration of La@N-P-CQDs against E. coli and S. aureus was 0.5 mg/mL. Similarly, the increase rate of absorbance at different concentrations was basically consistent after the cocultivation of different concentrations of N-P-CQDs with the same amount of E. coli and S. aureus for 24 h (Figures S11A, S11B). The result showed that N-P-CQDs had no obvious inhibitory effect on E. coli and S. aureus. Compared with that of the control group, the absorbance of the experimental group decreased sharply at first as the concentration of La@N-P-CQDs increased, indicating that N-P-CQDs@La NPs had a certain antibacterial effect (Fig. 3B, 3C). The increase of culture time resulted in a significantly lower growth rate of the absorbance in the experimental group than that of the control group. The absorbance of the experimental group remained low when the concentration of La@N-P-CQDs was 1.0 and 2.0 mg/mL, indicating that La@N-P-CQDs had an excellent antibacterial effect.

Fig. 3D and S11C show that the growth of E. coli and S. aureus in the medium containing N-P-CQDs was relatively good after 24 h cocultivation as the concentration increased. Compared with the blank group, the number of colonies did not change significantly. This finding indicated that N-P-CQDs had no effect on E. coli and S. aureus. The number of colonies in the medium containing La@N-P-CQDs decreased significantly as the concentration of La@N-P-CQDs increased. No bacterial growth could be observed on the surface of the solid medium when the concentration was greater than 0.5 mg/mL. This finding showed that La@N-P-CQDs had a good antibacterial effect when the concentration was greater than 0.5 mg/mL. The anionic microbial membrane was attracted by the cationic La@N-P-CQDs, causing the death of bacteria [38,39]. In all, La@N-P-CQDs had a more obvious antibacterial effect than N-P-CQDs.

Fig. 4 A shows that the weight loss of PVA film was relatively serious. The weight loss rate at 800 °C was 100%. The weight loss of the PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite film was mainly divided into four stages. The first stage, before 200 °C, was mainly caused by the residual water molecules of the composite film [40]. In the second stage, at 200 °C to 400 °C, the cross-linked segments of PVA and La@N-P-CQDs were broken; thus, large amounts of ammonia, nitrogen oxides, and carbon oxides gases were released, increasing sharply the weight loss rate of the composite film [41]. In the third stage, at 400 °C to 550 °C, the weight loss rate of the PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite film began to decrease slightly mainly because of the generation of La2O3 under high-temperature calcination. The further carbonization of the molecular chain was delayed. Thus, the weight loss rate decreased. In the fourth stage, at 550 °C to 800 °C, the PVA film continued to be carbonized further, whereas the composite film was stable. Finally, the residual carbon rates of PVA, PVA/N-P-CQD, and PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite films were 0%, 8.1%, and 10.2%, respectively, indicating that the addition of elements was beneficial to improve the thermal stability of PVA films. Figure S12 shows the fluorescence spectra of PVA, PVA/N-P-CQD, and PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite films. The fluorescence intensity of the composite films was obviously related to the amount of the N-P-CQDs added. The fluorescence intensity of the composite films reached the maximum when the amount of the N-P-CQD added was 0.2 w%. The increase of the amount of the added N-P-CQDs prompted the decrease of the fluorescence mainly because of too many agglomerated N-P-CQDs and fluorescence reabsorption during natural drying [35].

Contact angle is an essential means of detecting hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity. Fig. 4B shows the WCA of PVA, PVA/NP-CQD, and PVA/NP-CQDs@La composite films. The WCA of control group PVA was 52.35° mainly because PVA had more hydroxyl groups than PVA/NP-CQD and PVA/NP-CQDs@La. Thus, PVA had a certain degree of hydrophilicity. However, the hydrophilicity of PVA was improved after it compounded with N-P-CQDs. This process resulted in a large number of carboxyl groups, hydroxyl groups, and amino groups on the N-P-CQD surface. These hydrophilic groups further improved PVA hydrophilicity. The PVA/NP-CQDs@La composite film had a smaller WCA than PVA and PVA/NP-CQD. The WCA was 29.49° when 0.5 w% of La@N-P-CQDs was compounded with PVA. The reason is that La was an oxygen-philic element. The addition of La@N-P-CQDs further optimized the hydrophilic groups on the PVA surface. Thus, PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite film had excellent hydrophilicity and could be used in medical dressing applications, in which PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite films could keep the wound surface moist and reduce close adhesion to the wound.

N-P-CQDs surface contained a large number of hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups. During solution blending, N-P-CQDs could combine with PVA containing a large number of hydroxyl groups to form a composite film through hydrogen bonds. When this film is soaked in 37 °C PBS (pH = 7.4) solution, it would be released from PVA because of the excellent affinity with water of N-P-CQDs and La@N-P-CQDs. In the beginning, N-P-CQDs and La@N-P-CQDs only had a small amount of release. However, the release amount increased rapidly with the increase of time (Fig. 4C). The insets show that the fluorescence intensity of PVA/N-P-CQDs and PVA/La@N-P-CQDs had significantly reduced. This finding further indicated that N-P-CQDs and La@N-P-CQDs were nearly completely released in the PBS solution. Hence, N-P-CQDs and La@N-P-CQDs possessed not only ultrasmall size but also excellent hydrophilicity. They could also be quickly dissolved in water. Moreover, La@N-P-CQDs had better hydrophilicity than N-P-CQDs. Thus, the final cumulative release of PVA/La@N-P-CQDs was significantly greater than that of PVA/N-P-CQDs. Figure S10 also shows the cytotoxicity data statistics of PVA, PVA/N-P-CQD, and PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite films. The two composite films had no obvious toxicity, and the low concentration of La@N-P-CQDs had a certain promotion effect on cell proliferation, which was also related to the Hormesis effect of rare earth elements. In summary, PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite film had good biocompatibility and provided a moist environment for wounds. Thus, it could be used as a potential material for ideal medical dressings.

The typical SEM image shown in Fig. 4E revealed that the surface of PVA film adhered considerably to E. coli and S. aureus, and the shape was full, indicating that pure PVA film had no antibacterial effect. Compared with that on PVA film, the number of E. coli and S. aureus that adhered to and reproduced on the surface of the PVA/N-P-CQD composite film was slightly reduced. N-P-CQDs with good water solubility could be released when the PVA/N-P-CQD composite film was cocultured with E. coli and S. aureus. The N-P-CQDs were negatively charged, and E. coli and S. aureus were also negatively charged. Thus, the number of E. coli and S. aureus adhering on the surface of PVA/N-P-CQD composite film was reduced. In particular, the number of adherent and multiplying E. coli and S. aureus was significantly reduced. E. coli and S. aureus were destroyed on the surface of the PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite film. Given the excellent water solubility of the La@N-P-CQDs released from the PVA film surface, the anionic microbial membrane was attracted by the cationic La3+, causing the death of E. coli and S. aureus [42].

Fig. 5 A shows that the treatment cycle of mouse wounds is approximately 14 days; after which, relevant wound tissues and organs were sliced. Fig. 5B shows wound closure at different time points. The wound treated with PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite film healed better than that of the other three groups, which still showed evident defects after 14 days of treatment. The change in the wound area in Fig. 5C revealed that the wounds in PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite film had a smaller trauma size than those in the other three groups at all times. All the groups showed severe bacterial infection during the H&E stained experiments, and many inflammatory cells (red arrow) migrated to the wound site after 3 days of treatment (Figures S13, S14). However, PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite film had more fibroblast cells and fewer inflammatory cells than the control groups, PVA, and PVA/N-P-CQDs groups, resulting in the good antibacterial effect of La@N-P-CQDs [14]. All groups formed a layer of epithelium after 7 days of treatment. PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite film group exhibited more blood vessels and hair follicles (red arrow) than the other three groups. The maximum amount of mature hair follicles, blood vessels, thickened epidermis, and well-proliferated fibroblast after 14 days of treatment demonstrated the best wound healing effect of PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite film (Fig. 5D). H&E staining further explored the histological analysis of major organs after 14 days of treatment (Figure S15). The results indicated that no pathological changes were observed in the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney for the mice in all the groups. The in vivo antibacterial results indicated that the PVA/La@N-P-CQD composite film induced an apparent antibacterial effect but no obvious damage to major organs, revealing a potential platform for antibacterial treatment.

In summary, we had constructed a biocompatibility PVA/La@N-P-CQDs composites film as a multifunctional wound dressing, which had good antibacterial property and effective wound healing in a shorter period of time in contrast to traditional La composites [43–45]. The PVA/La@N-P-CQDs composites film was synthesized based on PVA film and La@N-P-CQDs NPs under hydrogen bonds interaction force. Compared with other La antibacterial composites [3,6,43], La@N-P-CQDs had a better release effect because of their size advantage, the obtained La@N-P-CQDs NPs antibacterial with the antibacterial efficacy ＞99.9 % at 0.5 mg/mL against S. aureus and E. coli bacteria. La@N-P-CQDs NPs in antibacterial composites film could immediately produce La3+ for antibacterial. Furthermore, PVA/La@N-P-CQDs composites film had good biocompatibility and hydrophilicity, which could effectively keep the wound moist for preventing wound infection and cause no side effects. In vivo wound therapeutic also demonstrated that the PVA/La@N-P-CQDs composites film showed the best treatment effect among the control and experimental groups. Our work revealed the mechanism of action of La polymers as antibacterial agents and explored their antibacterial experimental effects in vitro and in vivo, allowing researchers to better understand explore antibacterial applications of La-containing polymers. Future work should consider in more detail the antimicrobial effects of different forms of La, as well as in vivo antimicrobial effects in the presence of different dressing mixes.

Mingqian Wang: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Yutian Su: Resources, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Yihan Liu: Software, Formal analysis. Ying Liang: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Shishan Wu: Methodology. Ninglin Zhou: Methodology, Project administration. Jian Shen: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Foundation of Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.10.018.

ce:section.ce:para:
